forms.label: compound / possessive
forms.hindi: घर का / घर की / घर के
forms.roman: ghar kā / ghar kī / ghar ke

true_relatives.note: घर is the direct Hindi descendant of Sanskrit गृह, with the consonant cluster simplified through Prakrit
Identical in both form and meaning — shared inheritance from the same Sanskrit root
Parallel development from a shared Indo-Iranian root, reinforcing the idea of a sheltered dwelling

useful_coincidences.meaning: a room at the top of a house
useful_coincidences.note: Sounds somewhat like 'ghar' and is also a domestic space — helps anchor the 'house' meaning without any etymological link

sound_alikes.note: 'gar' as in 'garage' — a place where things are sheltered, just as a घर shelters a family
גר (gar) means 'he lives/dwells' — a helpful coincidence since घर is where one lives

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit root gṛha referred to a built dwelling; as the word moved through Prakrit the cluster simplified to 'ghar', keeping the core meaning intact.

etymology_story: The word goes back to Sanskrit गृह (gṛha), the standard word for a house or home.
As Sanskrit evolved into Prakrit, the consonant cluster gṛ simplified into the easier 'ghar'.
The form passed unchanged through Apabhraṃśa into modern Hindi as घर, carrying the full warmth of 'home' along with the literal meaning of 'house'.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable — said as one short, clear unit

tips.tip: pronounce the 'g' exactly as in 'garden', then end with a soft 'r' — the 'a' is open like in 'car'

memory_hook.primary: A warm, glowing house seen through a rain-streaked window — inside a family sits around a table, a single light bulb hanging overhead
memory_hook.connection: The compact one-syllable shape of घर mirrors how home is the simplest, most central concept in life — and 'ghar' rhymes with the English word 'far', reminding you that home is always where you measure distance from

examples.hindi: मेरा घर बहुत बड़ा है।
examples.romanisation: merā ghar bahut baṛā hai.
examples.english: My house is very big.
examples.literal: my house very big is

examples.hindi: तुम घर कब आओगे?
examples.romanisation: tum ghar kab āoge?
examples.english: When will you come home?
examples.literal: you home when will-come

examples.hindi: उनके घर में एक सुंदर बगीचा है।
examples.romanisation: unke ghar mẽ ek sundar bagīcā hai.
examples.english: There is a beautiful garden in their house.
examples.literal: their house in one beautiful garden is

examples.hindi: घर के बाहर एक पुराना दरवाज़ा था।
examples.romanisation: ghar ke bāhar ek purānā darvāzā thā.
examples.english: There was an old door outside the house.
examples.literal: house of outside one old door was

true_relatives.meaning: a place rich in flavours / a cooking room
true_relatives.note: रसोई descends from Sanskrit रसवती, literally 'that which has rasa (flavour/juice)' — the place where flavours are created

useful_coincidences.note: Both 'sauce' and रसोई share the idea of liquid flavour — रस means juice/flavour, and a sauce is the essence of kitchen cooking

sound_alikes.note: 'rasoī' sounds close to 'rosy' — imagine a rosy-cheeked cook in the kitchen

evolution.meaning: place rich in flavour / cooking room

root_breakdown.logic: The root rasa meant taste, juice, or essence — the kitchen is literally 'the place of flavours', where raw ingredients are transformed into something that has taste.

etymology_story: The word is built on Sanskrit रस (rasa), meaning juice, flavour, or the essential quality of something.
The suffix -vatī made it 'that which is full of rasa' — describing the kitchen as the room where taste is created.
Through Prakrit the form shortened to rasoī, arriving in Hindi as रसोई with the same core meaning: the room of flavours.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress falls on the middle syllable 'so'; the final 'ī' is a distinct long vowel

tips.tip: the 'so·ī' ending sounds close to 'soee' — almost like 'rosy' said quickly

memory_hook.primary: A stone kitchen with copper pots bubbling over a fire, aromatic steam curling up from the rasa — the juice — of a rich curry
memory_hook.connection: The image of steaming flavoured liquid (rasa) directly reflects the Sanskrit root of रसोई — the kitchen is the room where rasa is made

examples.hindi: रसोई में बहुत अच्छी खुशबू आ रही है।
examples.romanisation: rasoī mẽ bahut acchī khushbū ā rahī hai.
examples.english: A very nice smell is coming from the kitchen.
examples.literal: kitchen in very good smell coming is

examples.hindi: माँ रसोई में खाना बना रही हैं।
examples.romanisation: mā̃ rasoī mẽ khānā banā rahī haĩ.
examples.english: Mum is cooking in the kitchen.
examples.literal: mother kitchen in food making is

examples.hindi: हमारे घर की रसोई बहुत छोटी है।
examples.romanisation: hamāre ghar kī rasoī bahut choṭī hai.
examples.english: The kitchen in our house is very small.
examples.literal: our house of kitchen very small is

examples.hindi: रसोई का नल खराब हो गया है।
examples.romanisation: rasoī kā nal kharāb ho gayā hai.
examples.english: The kitchen tap has broken.
examples.literal: kitchen of tap broken become gone is

true_relatives.meaning: a room with a bath/shower and often a toilet
true_relatives.note: Direct borrowing from English — the Hindi word is a phonetic adaptation of the English original

sound_alikes.note: Essentially the same word — 'bāthrūm' is the Indianised pronunciation of 'bathroom'

root_breakdown.logic: Borrowed wholesale from English during the colonial period and later with Westernisation of Indian homes — the concept of a dedicated bathroom room was imported along with the word.

etymology_story: The Old English word bæth referred simply to bathing or the water used for bathing.
In English it combined with 'room' to describe a dedicated room for washing.
Hindi adopted the compound word bāthrūm directly, making it one of the clearest English loans in everyday Hindi domestic vocabulary.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the first syllable 'bāth'; the 'oo' in rūm is long

tips.tip: pronounced almost identically to English 'bathroom' — the main difference is a slightly longer 'aa' in the first syllable

memory_hook.primary: A tiled room with a large copper bucket of water, a neatly folded towel on the rack, and a bar of soap on the ledge — unmistakably a bathroom in an Indian home
memory_hook.connection: The image maps directly to an Indian-style बाथरूम — the bucket replaces the bathtub but the room is the same concept the English word describes

examples.hindi: बाथरूम में साबुन नहीं है।
examples.romanisation: bāthrūm mẽ sābun nahī̃ hai.
examples.english: There is no soap in the bathroom.
examples.literal: bathroom in soap not is

examples.hindi: बाथरूम का दरवाज़ा बंद था।
examples.romanisation: bāthrūm kā darvāzā band thā.
examples.english: The bathroom door was closed.
examples.literal: bathroom of door closed was

examples.hindi: मैंने बाथरूम में तौलिया छोड़ दिया।
examples.romanisation: maĩne bāthrūm mẽ tauliyā choṛ diyā.
examples.english: I left the towel in the bathroom.
examples.literal: I-by bathroom in towel leaving gave

examples.hindi: इस घर में दो बाथरूम हैं।
examples.romanisation: is ghar mẽ do bāthrūm haĩ.
examples.english: There are two bathrooms in this house.
examples.literal: this house in two bathroom are

true_relatives.note: शौचालय is a compound of शौच (purification) + आलय (place) — 'the place of purification'

useful_coincidences.note: The śau- sound in शौचालय vaguely echoes 'shower' — both relate to cleansing the body

sound_alikes.note: The first syllable 'śau' sounds close to 'show' — imagine a sign being shown at the entrance to the toilet facility

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit concept of śauca (ritual and physical cleanliness) was combined with ālaya (place) to create a formal, polite term for the place where bodily purification occurs — used in official, public, and written contexts.

etymology_story: The root śuc in Sanskrit means 'to be pure' or 'to purify'; from it came शौच (śauca), meaning purity and especially bodily cleanliness.
This was combined with आलय (ālaya, 'place' or 'abode') to form शौचालय — literally 'the abode of purification'.
In modern Hindi, शौचालय is the formal word for toilet, used on signs, in official settings, and in writing, while टॉयलेट is used in casual speech.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the first syllable 'śau'; the final syllable 'lay' is light

tips.tip: the first syllable 'śau' sounds like the English word 'show'

memory_hook.primary: A clean white-tiled room with a sign on the door showing a lotus flower — the Sanskrit symbol of purity — above the word शौचालय
memory_hook.connection: The lotus (symbol of purity, śauca) on the door sign directly encodes the Sanskrit root of the word — शौचालय is 'the place of purity'

examples.hindi: स्कूल में नया शौचालय बनाया गया।
examples.romanisation: skūl mẽ nayā śaucālay banāyā gayā.
examples.english: A new toilet was built in the school.
examples.literal: school in new toilet built was-made

examples.hindi: शौचालय का दरवाज़ा बंद है।
examples.romanisation: śaucālay kā darvāzā band hai.
examples.english: The toilet door is closed.
examples.literal: toilet of door closed is

examples.hindi: यहाँ सार्वजनिक शौचालय कहाँ है?
examples.romanisation: yahā̃ sārvajanik śaucālay kahā̃ hai?
examples.english: Where is the public toilet here?
examples.literal: here public toilet where is

examples.hindi: घर में दो शौचालय होने चाहिए।
examples.romanisation: ghar mẽ do śaucālay hone cāhie.
examples.english: There should be two toilets in the house.
examples.literal: house in two toilet being should

true_relatives.note: बगीचा is directly borrowed from Persian باغیچه — the diminutive form of باغ (bāgh), meaning 'garden' or 'orchard'
The root form — Hindi also uses बाग़ (bāg) independently for a large garden or park
Identical to Hindi बगीचा — shared borrowing from Persian into both languages

sound_alikes.note: The first syllable 'bag' sounds like English 'baggy' — picture a gardener in baggy trousers tending a बगीचा

root_breakdown.logic: The Persian word for garden (bāgh) had a diminutive form bāghīcha meaning 'little garden'; in Hindi this became the standard word for any domestic garden, large or small.

etymology_story: The Persian word باغ (bāgh) meant a garden or orchard — a walled, cultivated green space.
Its diminutive form باغیچه (bāghīcha) meant a small or personal garden.
This word entered Hindi through Urdu, softening slightly in pronunciation to become बगीचा — now the everyday Hindi word for any garden.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the middle syllable 'gī', which has a long vowel

tips.tip: the first syllable 'ba' sounds exactly like the start of 'baggy'

memory_hook.primary: A lush walled garden with Persian-style arched gateways, tiled pathways, and rose bushes in full bloom — the classic Mughal garden layout
memory_hook.connection: The Mughal garden image directly reflects the Persian origin of बगीचा — the word arrived in Hindi with the Mughal love of formal walled gardens

examples.hindi: हमारे घर का बगीचा बहुत सुंदर है।
examples.romanisation: hamāre ghar kā bagīcā bahut sundar hai.
examples.english: The garden of our house is very beautiful.
examples.literal: our house of garden very beautiful is

examples.hindi: बगीचे में गुलाब के फूल खिले हैं।
examples.romanisation: bagīce mẽ gulāb ke phūl khile haĩ.
examples.english: Roses have bloomed in the garden.
examples.literal: garden in rose of flowers bloomed are

examples.hindi: बच्चे बगीचे में खेल रहे हैं।
examples.romanisation: bacce bagīce mẽ khel rahe haĩ.
examples.english: The children are playing in the garden.
examples.literal: children garden in play doing are

examples.hindi: उस बगीचे में आम और नींबू के पेड़ थे।
examples.romanisation: us bagīce mẽ ām aur nīmbū ke peṛ the.
examples.english: That garden had mango and lemon trees.
examples.literal: that garden in mango and lemon of trees were
